claimed_contribution: Develops and tests an aggregate-level Bayesian Dynamic Linear Model, grounded in a Nerlove-Arrow leaky-bucket framework, to quantify spillover from generic to branded paid search via latent awareness.
Shows that direct exposure measures from paid search data (generic impressions and clicks) can be used to model awareness dynamics and branded search outcomes jointly.
Demonstrates how standalone paid search metrics can be adjusted for spillover effects using impulse-response analysis, changing the apparent economics of generic keywords.
context: The empirical setting is a paid search campaign for a major lodging chain, using daily keyword-level aggregates from Google and Yahoo! in 2004. Keywords are grouped into generic terms (no brand name) and branded terms (include the brand name).
design_and_data: The authors estimate a multivariate Bayesian Dynamic Linear Model with a latent awareness state that evolves with generic search activity and decays over time. Branded impressions, branded clicks, and branded reservations are modeled jointly as functions of lagged branded activity, day-of-week/month controls, and awareness. Data are daily aggregates including impressions, clicks, reservations, cost, and position for several hundred keywords; Google spans March 1-December 20, 2004 and Yahoo! spans May 6-August 31, 2004. They compare the DLM to ARDL alternatives using Bayes factors and out-of-sample MAPE, and test reverse spillover with a generic-side ARDL.
main_findings: Generic clicks significantly increase latent awareness, while generic impressions do not show a significant effect on awareness.
Awareness has a significant positive effect on branded impressions, branded clicks, and branded reservations, implying positive spillover from generic to branded search.
Spillover is asymmetric: branded search does not significantly affect generic search, and the awareness-based DLM outperforms ARDL benchmarks in-sample and out-of-sample.
one_line_summary: Using daily paid search data from a lodging chain, the paper shows with a Bayesian dynamic model that generic clicks create awareness that spills over to future branded search activity, while reverse spillover is not supported.
relation_to_prior_work: extends Nerlove-Arrow style leaky-bucket advertising models to paid search by treating awareness as a latent state driven by generic search exposures and linked to branded search outcomes. It also adds empirical paid-search evidence in marketing where the paper states published empirical work was largely absent.
research_gap: The paper argues that managers lack validated, model-based methods to evaluate paid search campaigns when generic and branded keywords interact over time. Existing standalone metrics and sparse conversion data at the keyword-day level do not capture potential dynamic spillover from generic to branded search.
research_question: Does generic paid search activity create spillover to later branded paid search activity, and can that spillover be captured through a latent awareness process? The paper also asks whether spillover is bidirectional or asymmetric between generic and branded search, and whether an awareness-based dynamic model outperforms direct lag-based alternatives.
